questions.question: What does `jekyll build` do to the existing `_site/` directory at the start of a run?
choices: Appends new files only - leaves existing output untouched
Wipes it and rebuilds the whole site from source
Renames it to `_site.bak/` and creates a fresh copy
Compares timestamps and rewrites only changed files by default
questions.explanation: By default, `jekyll build` clears `_site/` and writes a complete fresh build. Anything in `_site/` that didn't come from the current build is gone - which is why you never edit `_site/` directly. (Incremental mode is the opt-in exception.)

questions.question: You enable `--incremental` while iterating on a post. After editing `_layouts/post.html`, the rebuilt post in `_site/` still shows the old layout. What's happening?
choices: Incremental's dependency tracking misses some layout/include/config changes, so it doesn't rebuild dependent files
`--incremental` only works with the kramdown converter disabled
questions.explanation: Incremental builds use a stored dependency graph that's imperfect for layouts, includes, data, and config. Stale output is the classic symptom. Drop `--incremental` for a full rebuild before trusting the result.

questions.question: Your local build succeeds, but pushing to GitHub Pages fails because a Jekyll plugin you added isn't allowed. Which response best fits the trade-offs covered in this chapter?
choices: Roll back the plugin - GitHub Pages is the source of truth for what Jekyll can do
Replace the plugin with raw Liquid in every post that uses it
Switch to a different SSG that allows more plugins
Either pick a safelisted plugin, or build with GitHub Actions so the safelist no longer applies
questions.explanation: GitHub Pages' default Jekyll build only permits a fixed plugin safelist. The two real options are conforming to the safelist or moving the build into GitHub Actions, which runs your own Jekyll without restrictions. Module 5 covers the Actions path.
